file_name: Islam et al. - 2024 - P028 The importance of clinical metadata when triaging patients for face-to-face assessment and poss.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (ML) models, specifically naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine, random forest, and multilayer perceptron, to classify suspicious skin lesions based on patient metadata (including 7PCL and Williams score) for triaging patients in a teledermatology pathway.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is labeled as 'Abstracts' and 'P028', indicating it is a conference abstract. However, it reports original experimental results from a retrospective analysis of clinical metadata from 53,601 skin lesions using machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, etc.) to classify suspicious lesions. According to the instructions, conference abstracts with original results should be classified as Research paper.

title: The importance of clinical metadata when triaging patients for face-to-face assessment and possible biopsy in a teledermatology pathway
brief_summary: This study investigated the utility of clinical metadata for triaging patients with suspicious skin lesions in a teledermatology pathway to optimize face-to-face assessment and biopsy decisions. Using data from 53,601 skin lesions in 25,105 patients from private UK clinics (2015-2022), the researchers applied five machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, SVM, random forest, and multilayer perceptron) to classify lesions as suspicious or non-suspicious. The evaluation compared the performance of the standard 7-point checklist (7PCL) against models incorporating additional meta-features such as the Williams score, patient demographics, and lesion characteristics. Results showed that while the original 7PCL yielded a sensitivity of 68.1%, adding 11 specific meta-features significantly improved performance to a sensitivity of 85.2%. The findings suggest that integrating these high-performing metadata features can enhance ML model accuracy for skin cancer detection and potentially inform referral guidelines.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary classification task was to categorize lesions as 'suspicious' (10% of dataset) or 'non-suspicious' (90% of dataset) based on clinical metadata for triaging patients for face-to-face assessment and possible biopsy. The study does not report specific histopathological diagnoses for the 53,601 lesions used in the ML models, but focuses on the binary suspicious/non-suspicious label derived from specialist triage.

Mentioned in the context of NICE guidelines for urgent referral of pigmented lesions and as one of the incidental lesions identified in the separate P029 abstract (1 case of malignant melanoma). Not explicitly reported as a specific class label within the 53,601 lesion dataset of P028.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where the model predicts whether a skin lesion is 'suspicious' (10% of dataset) or 'non-suspicious' (90% of dataset) based on clinical metadata inputs (including 7PCL, Williams score, patient demographics, and lesion characteristics) to aid in triaging patients for face-to-face assessment and biopsy.

datasets_used.name: Private UK Teledermatology Clinic Dataset
datasets_used.description: Retrospective dataset of clinical metadata from patients attending private skin cancer diagnosis clinics in the UK between 2015 and 2022. The data includes patient demographics, lesion characteristics, and triage scores (7PCL, Williams score) collected via a teledermatology pathway.

use.training: Used as input features for five machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine, random forest, and multilayer perceptron) to classify lesions.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance (sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, AUC) comparing 7PCL alone versus 7PCL with additional meta-features.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of the original 7PCL against the weighted 7PCL and models with added meta-features for classifying suspicious skin lesions.

To determine if the performance gain from adding meta-features was statistically significant, as the paper states 'performance of the ML model was improved significantly' and 'no significant differences observed' between 7PCL and weighted 7PCL.

hypothesis_testing.test: Not explicitly named (likely statistical comparison of sensitivity metrics, e.g., McNemar's test or DeLong's test, though not specified)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in model performance (sensitivity) between the original 7PCL and the weighted 7PCL.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in model performance (sensitivity) between the original 7PCL and the weighted 7PCL (or between 7PCL and models with added meta-features).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No significant differences were observed in model performance between the 7PCL and weighted 7PCL (sensitivity 68.1%). However, the performance of the ML model was improved significantly for each added meta-feature, with the best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) observed when 11 specific meta-features were added to the 7PCL.

Private UK Teledermatology Clinic Datase.notes: The study compared the performance of the original 7-point checklist (7PCL) against a weighted 7PCL and machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine, random forest, and multilayer perceptron) incorporating additional meta-features. There were no significant differences observed in model performance between the 7PCL and weighted 7PCL (sensitivity 68.1%). However, the performance of the ML model was improved significantly for each added meta-feature. The best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) was observed when 11 specific meta-features were added to the 7PCL: lesion pink, Williams score, lesion age, sunburn, patient age, Williams group, patient sex, hair colour, site of the lesion on the body, freckling tendency, and mole count.

architecture_used.main_model: Five machine learning models: naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine (SVM), random forest, and multilayer perceptron.

input_features: 7PCL (change of lesion size, shape, colour, lesion > 7 mm, inflamed, oozing, itching)
best_feature_subset: site of the lesion on the body

conclusions: There were no significant differences in model performance (sensitivity 68.1%) between the original 7-point checklist (7PCL) and the weighted 7PCL for classifying suspicious skin lesions.
The performance of machine learning models was significantly improved for each added meta-feature beyond the 7PCL.

The best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) was observed when 11 specific meta-features were added to the 7PCL: lesion pink, Williams score, lesion age, sunburn, patient age, Williams group, patient sex, hair colour, site of the lesion on the body, freckling tendency, and mole count.
The study identified an optimal subset of meta-features that improve ML model performance for categorizing suspicious skin lesions during telemedicine triage compared with 7PCL alone.
Fusing these high-performing meta-features with image modalities is likely to further boost ML model performance for skin cancer detection.
The identified high-performing meta-features could potentially be used to modify current skin cancer referral guidelines.
important_remarks: The study is a conference abstract (P028) reporting on a retrospective analysis of clinical metadata from private UK teledermatology clinics (2015-2022), which limits generalizability to public NHS pathways or other healthcare systems.

The dataset consists of 53,601 lesions from 25,105 patients, with a significant class imbalance: only 10% of lesions were labeled 'suspicious' and 90% 'non-suspicious' based on specialist triage, not histopathology.

The 'suspicious' label was derived from telemedicine triage decisions (cancer detection rate = 5%), meaning the ground truth reflects clinical suspicion rather than confirmed malignancy, potentially introducing label noise.

The study utilized five machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, SVM, random forest, multilayer perceptron) but does not report detailed performance metrics (e.g., AUC, specificity) for individual models, only aggregate sensitivity improvements.

The best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) was achieved by adding 11 specific meta-features to the 7PCL, but the paper does not report confidence intervals or p-values for these improvements, only stating they were 'significant'.
The data source is exclusively private UK clinics, which may introduce selection bias regarding patient demographics, skin types, and referral patterns compared to general population or NHS cohorts.
The abstract mentions funding by Innovate UK and the private teledermatology pathway provider, indicating potential industry affiliation that could influence study design or reporting.
The study does not provide external validation results; findings are based on a single retrospective dataset from one provider network.
The paper suggests fusing these metadata features with image modalities for future work, but does not present any multimodal model results in this abstract.
